title: What The Body Does With Unspoken No
dek: When you swallow a "no," it doesn't disappear. It lodges in the masseter, tightens the psoas, and constricts the diaphragm. This is not pathology; this is biology. Here is how the body archives your unspoken boundaries and how to begin releasing them.
bodyMarkdown:
It’s 3 PM on a Tuesday, the kind of late afternoon where the sun slants through the window at a brittle angle, casting long shadows across your desk. You’re sitting upright, but your jaw clenches without your permission—a subtle tightening of the masseter muscle, that thick band running from your cheekbone to your lower jaw. You don’t even realize it at first, this clench is so automatic, so ingrained. Your teeth press together softly but insistently, a silent sentinel guarding a no you never said out loud.

The breath you take is shallow, barely filling the dome of your diaphragm, which sits low behind your ribs. Instead of that deep, nourishing inhale that expands your belly and relaxes your shoulders, you draw in a quick, tight sip of air. Your trapezius muscles hitch upward, that familiar tension knitting between your neck and your shoulders, like a coil ready to spring. The levator scapulae, those thin strands running up the sides of your neck, stiffen, bracing against a threat only you feel. You think you’re holding yourself together, but what you’re really doing is holding the ‘no’ inside, swallowing it whole as though it were something bitter and indigestible.

This is not pathology; this is biology. The body knows every no you don’t say. It doesn’t forget, doesn’t let go with ease. Instead, it stores these unspoken boundaries in the fascia—the dense, fibrous connective tissue that envelopes every muscle and organ. That quiet ‘no’ becomes a knot in the masseter, a constriction in the diaphragm, a stubborn tightness in the trapezius. It’s a living ledger of every time you’ve been told to be ‘nice’ or ‘quiet,’ every moment your fury was deemed too much, every demand on your time or energy you hesitated to refuse.

You might not be able to say the word ‘no’ in that moment, but your body says it for you, and it does so with a clenched jaw, a shallow breath, a shoulder pulled tight against the weight of unclaimed boundaries. This is the anatomy of an unspoken no. It’s written in the muscles, the breath, the posture, in the very way you inhabit your skin at 3 PM on a Tuesday. And if you begin to listen—to feel where the body stores this tension—you can start to uncoil those silent refusals and reclaim the space you’ve quietly ceded.

## The Psoas and the Flight Response

You know that restless stirring deep in your belly, the one that wakes you at 3 a.m. when the world is silent and the air smells of damp earth and old wood? That’s your psoas muscle speaking, holding tight the energy of every ‘no’ you never said out loud. The psoas isn’t just some abstract core muscle; it’s the body’s primal gatekeeper, the thick, tender cord that runs from your lumbar spine, threads through the pelvic bowl, and anchors into your femur. It’s the muscle that literally carries you forward—or doesn’t—when you need to flee.

Bessel van der Kolk, who’s spent decades listening to what trauma does to the body, reminds us that the body keeps the score in ways the mind cannot always grasp. This isn’t about pathology or some new-age metaphor. It is biology. When the urge to say ‘no’—to push back, to escape, to refuse—is stifled, that energy doesn’t just vanish. It pools in the psoas and the fascia around your pelvic bowl, like a river dammed behind a cracked wall. This fascia, that dense, slippery sheath of connective tissue, remembers tension long after your mind has moved on. It’s where your fight-or-flight response gets caught, stuck in a liminal state, neither running nor resting.

When you were taught your fury was ugly, that ‘no’ was unsafe, your psoas learned to coil like a spring under constant compression. The muscle’s fibers, like taut violin strings, shorten and harden, holding you in a kind of bodily silence. You might notice this as a dull ache in the lower back, a tightness behind your belly button, or a peculiar heaviness in the front of your hips. The psoas doesn’t just tighten; it contracts the entire pelvic bowl, shifting your center of gravity forward and pulling you into a guarded posture. This is your body’s way of saying, “I am ready to run—but I can’t.”

This tension can show up as restless nights, the kind where your breath feels shallow and the diaphragm fights for space beneath ribcage and lungs. As you lie awake, you might sense the psoas gripping like a fist around the femur, unwilling or unable to release. The fascia, that intricate network enveloping the psoas, thickens with unspoken resistance and unmet boundaries. Your body remembers every moment you swallowed your ‘no’ like a bitter root, every time you folded your anger into quiet compliance.

In my work with women who’ve been taught their fury was ugly, I often return to the teachings of Thomas Hanna, who emphasized that the psoas is the “muscle of the soul,” the gatekeeper of survival. It’s not just a muscle but a repository of embodied history. The trapped flight impulse in the pelvic bowl is not a flaw; it’s an archive of self-preservation. When you begin to listen—through breath, movement, and sometimes the gentle uncoiling hands of herbal medicine—you start to unravel the fascia’s tight grip. The body’s biology unfolds, releasing what was never meant to stay.

The psoas, that stubborn, tender muscle, waits patiently beneath layers of shame and silence. It holds your unspoken ‘no’ with all its ache and wisdom, ready to teach you how to move again—on your own terms.

## The Throat and the Vagus Nerve

You know that tightness in your throat that lingers long after you’ve bitten your tongue? That slow, curling coil of constriction around your vocal cords, like a noose tied too tight, too often? That’s your body carrying the weight of the no you didn’t say. The masseter muscle clenches in your jaw, sure, but it’s deeper, softer tissues—the delicate muscles that open and close your larynx—that bear the brunt when your voice is stifled. The vocal cords themselves, those twin folds of mucous membrane, contract reflexively, shutting down the possibility of sound, of refusal, of boundary.

This tightness isn’t random. It’s a somatic signature of a nervous system caught between fight, flight, and freeze. The vagus nerve, that wandering giant of the autonomic nervous system, threads through your neck like a highway, whispering to your heart, lungs, and digestive tract. When you suppress your no, the vagus nerve tightens its grip, constricting not just the physical space in your throat but the subtle energetic flow that governs expression and connection. It’s not just metaphor—this is biology.

Gabor Maté’s work sheds light on how this suppression of emotion—especially around boundaries—isn’t just psychological. It imprints on your tissues. The thyroid gland, nestled just beneath the Adam’s apple, takes the brunt. It’s exquisitely sensitive, a small butterfly-shaped organ that controls your metabolism, your energy, your very sense of self in the world. When the vagus nerve is chronically overstimulated by unvoiced no’s, the thyroid’s delicate fascia and blood supply become compromised. Autoimmune responses can flare, a body’s desperate, messy attempt to communicate a boundary it never got to say aloud.

You might notice this constriction most acutely in the deep quiet of early morning—3 a.m., when the world is still and your breath is the loudest sound you hear. The levator scapulae, those muscles at the back of your neck that cradle your skull, begin to ache, a dull throb that runs into the trapezius and down to the diaphragm. Your breath feels shallow, caught high in your throat, the diaphragm’s full, expansive movement stifled by the locked vocal cords. You’re not imagining this resistance; your body remembers every no withheld.

This is not pathology. This is biology. The body encodes every unspoken refusal, every boundary ignored, and it responds in muscle, nerve, gland. It remembers in the fascia that wraps the thyroid, in the tension lodged in the cricoid and arytenoid cartilages that pull your vocal cords taut. It knows before your mind does. When you finally give that no a voice, you feel it release—a softening in the throat, a loosening of the vagus’s grip, the diaphragm’s sigh widening into a full exhale. This is the body reclaiming what was always yours: the right to speak, to refuse, to protect your space with sound.

## Herbal Allies for the Unspoken No

The body carries the unspoken no like a secret ledger inked into muscle and fascia. When you swallow your refusal, when you soften your edges to keep the peace, the masseter muscle clenches just beneath your earlobe, the trapezius tightens along the ridge of your shoulders, and the levator scapulae knots up like a tangled thread between your neck and collarbone. This is not pathology, this is biology. Your nervous system is wired to register threat, and when your boundaries are continuously crossed, it stays on high alert, like a night watchman who never gets a break. This is where herbal allies step in—not as gentle flavoring agents, but as resolute medicine that understands the weight of your body’s archive.

Wood Betony, Stachys officinalis, is a root you want to know by heart if your no remains locked behind clenched teeth and a throat that won’t open. Sitting with the herb, you notice how its essence draws down into the diaphragm, releasing the tension lodged in the solar plexus and the subtle clench of the jaw. The psoas, that deep core muscle often stiffening in response to emotional constriction, softens as the Wood Betony’s bitter compounds encourage grounding. It is a medicine that walks the line between calming and strengthening, not unlike the teacher Stephen Buhner describes—one who insists you meet your own edge rather than skirt around it. The body knows this medicine; it recognizes the invitation to root deeper rather than flee.

Then there is Blue Vervain, Verbena hastata, a fierce ally for the tight, screaming levator scapulae and the stubborn trapezius knots that flare every time you brace against the world’s demands. This is not a tea to sip lightly. The herb’s bitter, astringent bite works on the subtle fascia restrictions around the cervical spine, coaxing the muscles to relax and the lymphatic flow to resume its necessary circulation. Blue Vervain’s nervine qualities soothe the sympathetic nervous system’s overdrive, that relentless urge to tense up, to stand guard. It’s the medicine that helps you say no not just with words but with a body that stops trembling in refusal. Herbalist Matthew Wood, who has long respected Vervain’s capacity to work deeply on the nervous system, reminds us that this is a medicine for the warrior who is exhausted not by battle but by the need to pretend she was never fighting at all.

Both these herbs demand your respect. They are not quick fixes or mere comforts. They arrive in your body like old friends who recall the stories you forgot you told yourself. When your no is unspoken, when the boundaries are invisible yet impenetrable in the body’s memory, Wood Betony and Blue Vervain offer a path to reclaim your voice—not only through speech but through the relaxation of the diaphragm, the unwinding of the neck’s tension, the softening of the clenched masseter. They remind you that the throat remembers, the jaw remembers, and the body always knows.

## The Liver and Anger in TCM

The body is never silent about what you refuse to say. That unspoken no lodges itself deep inside, and in Traditional Chinese Medicine, the liver is the organ that carries this particular weight. Not just the liver you know from anatomy class, but the Liver as a dynamic, moving force within your body’s energetic landscape. It governs the smooth flow of qi—the vital energy coursing through your vessels, muscles, and fascia—and when you clamp down on your no, when you swallow your anger and don’t let it ripple outward, the Liver qi stagnates. This is not some abstract metaphor; it is a living, breathing reality that your muscles, nerves, and connective tissue know intimately.

Imagine it is 2 a.m., and you lie awake in the damp chill of a late spring night. The air smells faintly of wet earth and broken dandelions, and your masseter muscles are clenched so tightly they throb with a dull ache, like a low drumbeat inside your jaw. The fascia around your temples feels thickened, almost gluey, as though the connective tissue itself has lost its fluid ease. This is Liver qi rebellion—stagnation manifesting as tension headaches that wrap around your skull like a vice, as if your head is trying to squeeze the truth out of your clenched teeth.

I learned this from my teacher Giovanni Maciocia, who always said, “The liver’s role is movement—of qi, of blood, of emotions—and when it is blocked, the body rebels in pain and rigidity.” The jaw remembers what you refuse to say. Those tight, grinding contractions in the masseter and temporalis muscles are the body’s way of holding the line, a somatic “no” carved into the very fascia of your face. It’s not just tension; it’s the body’s archive of your silenced fury.

This stagnation isn’t pathology—it’s biology. The trapezius and levator scapulae muscles hitch up toward your ears with a sharpness that mimics the emotional snap you dare not voice. Your diaphragm, that great bellows of breath, tightens in response, constricting your ability to exhale fully, trapping the anger inside like smoke in a closed room. The liver’s qi wants to move; it is designed for flow. When you suppress your no, you starve this flow, and your body responds with these unmistakable signals—headaches, jaw clenching, a restless tightness along your ribs where the liver sits, just beneath your right lower ribs.

Herbs like Chai Hu (Bupleurum) and formulas such as Xiao Yao San are time-honored allies in these moments, working to soothe and gently move stuck Liver qi. But before the herbs can do their work, you must witness the body’s language—the subtle shiver in the psoas when anger twists down into your belly, the way your jaw tightens like a trap at midnight. This is your body speaking the no you could not say aloud. Listen to it. The body knows.

## Somatic Practice: The Jaw Release

The jaw remembers. It is one of the first places your body archives the unspoken no—the tight, clenched refusal you never let out loud. When you hold back that no, the masseter muscle, those thick bands running from your cheekbones to your lower jaw, lock down like a fortress. The levator scapulae, those small but fierce muscles running from the upper cervical vertebrae to the top of your shoulder blades, join the siege, pulling your neck into a guarded, almost defensive posture. This is not pathology; this is biology. Your body is simply doing what it knows: holding tension where words have been swallowed.

You’ll want to find a quiet moment, perhaps just before dawn when the air is cool and still and the world hasn’t yet demanded your compliance. Sit upright in a chair with your feet planted firmly on the floor. Let your shoulders drop, but notice if the levator scapulae are still clutching your neck just behind the ears. Place your fingers gently on the masseter, just above your jawline, where you can feel the muscle bulge when you clench your teeth. Take a deep breath and exhale slowly, softening your jaw almost immediately.

Now, begin a slow, deliberate massage on the masseter. Use your fingertips to press with firm but tender pressure, moving in small circles along the length of the muscle. You’ll notice the fascia—this dense, fibrous webbing—may feel sticky or tight. That’s the unspoken no, frozen in place. As you work, angle your fingers toward the back of the jaw and the ear, where the tension often lodges stubbornly. Don’t rush this. The muscle fibers need to be coaxed, not forced. If you feel a sharp pinch, ease up. This is a conversation, not a confrontation.

After several minutes, shift your attention to the levator scapulae. Reach behind your neck with your opposite hand, sliding your fingers beneath the base of your skull to the top of your shoulder blade. This muscle often tightens when you’re guarding not just your words but your very presence. With your thumb or fingertips, apply slow, steady pressure, pausing on spots that feel knotted or resistant. As you do this, tilt your head gently away from the side you’re working on—this stretches the muscle and invites it to release. Breathe deeply into your ribs, allowing the diaphragm to expand, because the diaphragm’s movement can signal the neck and jaw to let go.

You might notice a subtle ache or a dull warmth spreading through the area. This is your body waking up to what it’s been holding. Maybe there’s a faint scent of your skin—sweat, maybe, or the faint musk of evening herbs you rubbed on earlier. This is the body’s language of unspoken no, finally given space to soften. When you finish, rest your hands in your lap and let your jaw hang loose, the lower edge of your teeth just barely touching. Notice how the weight of the unspoken no shifts, how the body feels less braced, less resigned.

This practice is simple, but it requires your presence and your permission to feel what’s been silenced. The jaw and neck do not lie—their tension is truth told in muscle and fascia. You are not undoing damage; you are honoring biology.

## The Cost of the Good Girl

You learn early that your no is unwelcome, that it’s a bitter taste in the mouths of those around you. The girl who says no too often, too firmly, too loudly—she gets labeled difficult, unpleasant, unlovable. So you swallow your no. You tuck it under your tongue like a bitter root, chew it into silence, and carry on. This swallowing isn’t abstract. It happens in the grinding of your masseter muscles, tight and clenched as you force a smile. It lives in the subtle stiffening of your trapezius as you lift your shoulders toward your ears, bracing against invisible blows. The body keeps the score, and the good girl’s cost is written in fascia and muscle.

Resmaa Menakem, a somatic abolitionist whose work I return to often, teaches us that trauma is not just in the mind but seared into the body’s tissues. The same holds for the trauma of swallowing your no. Menakem points out that this isn’t just individual—it’s collective, ancestral, racialized. The body carries the burden of generations who learned to silence themselves to survive. You might notice it in the levator scapulae, that muscle along the side of your neck, tightening every time you anticipate conflict. Your diaphragm, the breath’s muscle, catches and stutters, holding your exhale hostage because speaking up feels unsafe. This is not pathology, this is biology.

The exhaustion of 'being nice' is not just mental fatigue but visceral depletion. Imagine late at night, the kind of night where the air smells faintly of wet earth and woodsmoke, your body finally unclenching only to realize it’s been holding tension all day. The psoas, often called the muscle of the soul, clenched in a low-level panic you didn’t name. Your jaw remembers every word unsaid and every boundary dissolved. This is the quiet violence of the good girl script: the energy it takes to keep your body in a state of readiness, to suppress your own needs and soften your edges. You think you’re just being polite; your body knows you’re fighting for survival.

This conditioning doesn’t just vanish with awareness. It takes time and patience to unravel the knotted fascia of compliance and rewrite what your muscles expect. You can feel it in the slow unwinding of your trapezius as you allow yourself to say no, the gentle release of your diaphragm as you breathe into your right to refuse. It’s an unlearning that requires fierce tenderness, sometimes with the help of herbs that steady the nerves, like chamomile’s gentle calm or skullcap’s cooling balm. The body knows. The jaw remembers. And it’s ready to unlearn.

## Reclaiming the No

You know that moment before the word even forms, that split second when your body stiffens, and your breath catches somewhere behind your diaphragm? That is the body’s no, long before the throat tries to articulate it, before the voice even flickers to life. It’s subtle—often so subtle you might miss it if you’re not paying close attention. The masseter tightens, a low-grade clench that isn’t quite a headache but signals your jaw remembers tension. Your trapezius, that broad, flat muscle spanning from neck to shoulder, lifts just a fraction, as if bracing for something you’re not ready to say out loud. These micro-movements are the body’s first line of defense, a whisper of refusal that the mind often overrides or ignores.

You were taught your fury was ugly, and so you learned to swallow the no, burying it deep until it takes up residence in the levator scapulae, that small muscle at the back and side of your neck, knitting into a tight ball that wakes you in the middle of a damp, restless night. You might wake with a dry mouth, the taste of unspoken words lingering like an old herb tincture gone bitter on your tongue. The body does not deceive; it archives these small refusals in fascia and muscle, in the slow, silent tightening of the psoas as you hold your breath—sometimes literally—as if trying to fold yourself small enough to disappear.

Before the no reaches your throat, before your tongue twists itself into silence, start listening to this subtle language. The body’s no isn’t loud or dramatic. It’s a soft, insistent pressure behind the sternum, the slight withdrawal of your diaphragm, a hesitation in your exhale as if you’re holding back your very life force. This is not pathology; this is biology. The body knows before the mind catches up. It’s not about forcing a word but honoring what your muscles are already saying. When you feel the no in your diaphragm tightening, when your masseter clenches as you resist a request, pause there. Let your awareness settle into that texture of refusal, that somatic punctuation mark.

Honoring these boundaries means listening to the body’s early warnings, not waiting for the throat to scream or the jaw to ache with clenched rage. It means recognizing that the no lives in your tissues, that it’s a living thing embedded in the subtle contractures and releases of your muscles. When you begin to feel the no before it’s spoken, you reclaim a vital part of your sovereignty. You begin to rewrite the story that your fury is ugly by showing your muscles that their refusal is not only valid but necessary. The body remembers this truth long after words have failed. You just have to learn to read its language.

## FAQ

**Q:** Why does my jaw ache and my neck feel like stone after I say yes when I want to say no?

**A:** The masseter and the levator scapulae muscles are the usual culprits here. When you swallow down a “no,” the jaw locks into subtle clenching—maybe you don’t even realize you’re grinding your teeth, but the fascia around your masseter thickens, the fibers shorten, and the jaw remembers this tension in its quiet, persistent way. Meanwhile, the levator scapulae, that muscle running from your neck vertebrae to your shoulder blade, tightens to brace against the discomfort of unreleased boundaries. This isn’t just metaphor; it’s biology. The body translates your unspoken refusal into a physical contracture, like a silent scream held in the sinew. You might notice it most late at night, around 3 or 4 a.m., when the world is quiet and your body’s defenses lower. That’s when the tension blooms, and you wake with a grinding headache or a stiff neck. This is not pathology; this is the body’s way of saying, “I am not okay with this.”

**Q:** How can herbs help when I’m exhausted from constantly suppressing my anger?

**A:** When fury is stuffed away, it doesn’t vanish—it festers in the liver’s fascia and the diaphragm’s rhythmic dance. The liver, in Chinese medicine and Western herbalism alike, governs the smooth flow of qi and blood. When you suppress your “no,” that flow stagnates, and the diaphragm—your breath’s gatekeeper—becomes tight, shallow, and uneven. I lean on teachers like Jill Stansbury and her wisdom about bupleurum and peony, herbs that move the liver qi gently, like a soft wind clearing tension from the branches. These herbs are not magic spells; they are carefully wrought medicines that speak to the muscle and fascia, coaxing them to relax and release. Chamomile and skullcap support the nervous system, tempering the exhaustion from emotional suppression, while ginger warms and moves stagnation. Taken as a tea before bed, these herbs can soften the diaphragm’s cage, allowing you to breathe deeper, to inhabit your ribcage fully, to reclaim the space your unspoken “no” has been denying you.

**Q:** Why do my shoulders and upper back seize up when I avoid confrontation?

**A:** The trapezius muscle, that broad, powerful sheet spanning your neck to mid-back, is a chronic holder of unexpressed boundary stress. When you avoid saying “no,” the trapezius braces your upper body, pulling your shoulders up and forward, creating that “hunched, weighed-down” feeling that lingers into the afternoon. The fascia around this muscle densifies, almost like a protective armor that becomes its own cage. It’s common to feel this as a dull ache, a knot you can’t quite massage away, or a sharp bite when you turn your head. The body is manifesting your boundary violation physically because it cannot tolerate the emotional compression in silence. Somatic practices that invite you to gently roll your shoulders back, to lengthen the trapezius fibers slowly and with attention, help. I often guide women through slow, weighted shoulder shrugs combined with breath held in the diaphragm, the exhale letting go of tension. This is not about forcing the muscle but inviting it to remember softness.

**Q:** What somatic practices can help me embody my “no” without fear?

**A:** The body remembers everything, especially the jaw’s clenches, the psoas’s tightness, the diaphragm’s shallow lifts. To embody your “no,” you must first meet the body where it holds the memory of suppression. I borrow from the teachings of Peter Levine and his work with the vagus nerve and trauma release, which has you track the small shifts in the diaphragm and the psoas muscle, the deep hip flexor that tightens when you hold in rebellion. A practice might look like slow, intentional diaphragmatic breathing combined with gentle hip openers—simple lunges or supported pigeon poses—to soften the psoas. As you breathe, you say the “no” silently, feeling how the breath moves through the tightness, how your ribs expand and the jaw unclenches. This is not performance; it is listening. The body knows. It remembers. Somatic work is a conversation, not a conquest.

**Q:** How do I know if my physical symptoms are from suppressed boundaries and not something else?

**A:** This is a crucial question because pain and tension can have many sources. The difference lies in the story your body tells and the context in which symptoms arise. If your jaw tightens specifically after conversations where you feel pressured, or your shoulders seize up after you avoid confrontation, that’s a strong indicator that your body is encoding unspoken “no.” Notice the timing: Does your neck stiffness spike when you’re around certain people or situations? Do your headaches coincide with moments when you swallow your truth? This pattern recognition is the key. The body doesn’t lie, but it also doesn’t diagnose. If you have other symptoms—numbness, weakness, or systemic issues—seek medical advice. But if the pain is muscular, localized in the masseter, trapezius, levator scapulae, or the diaphragm feels restricted, and it correlates with emotional suppression, you’re likely witnessing the somatic imprint of your unspoken boundaries. This is biology, not shame. The body is simply telling you what the mind won’t say.

bottomProducts.title: Garden of Life Organics B-12 Spray (Methylcobalamin)
